entry.title: Range Compress for dump SAPSQL_STMNT_TOO_LARGE
entry.content:
Introduction

When the size of the Range (as Selection Condition) is too big, system gives short-dump SAPSQL_STMNT_TOO_LARGE.

This function module will decrease the range lines without loosing data.

 

Details

SQL statements has a size limit for Condition Data. When ranges with line more than ~30.000 are used, system can not transfer the condition data to the Database and gives short-dump SAPSQL_STMNT_TOO_LARGE. 

 

For this issue "for all entries" statement is proposed but this statement causes performance problems.

 

The best way is to re-orginize the range data. If multiple line values are in an order, then it can be descreased to one line with operator "Between".

 

Range Compres function module will do this summerization automatically.

 

Example:

Range data Before Compress:

Sign        Option   Low        High

I              EQ          100021 

I              EQ          100024 

I              EQ          100023 

I              EQ          100022 

I              EQ          100028 

I              EQ          100034 

I              EQ          100030 

I              EQ          100031 

I              EQ          100035 

I              EQ          100026 

I              EQ          100037 

I              EQ          100036 

 

 

 Range data After Compress:

Sign        Option   Low        High

I              BT           100021  100024

I              EQ          100026 

I              EQ          100028 

I              BT           100030  100031

I              BT           100034  100037

 

Function Module Coding

This function module is dynamic and can be used for any type of range.

 

FUNCTION zpp_range_compress.
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
*"*"Local Interface:
*"  TABLES
*"      T_ITAB
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
  DATA : lrs_itab TYPE REF TO data, " Local Ref. Structure
         lrt_itab TYPE REF TO data, " Local Ref. Table
         lrv_low  TYPE REF TO data, " Local Ref. Variable
         lrv_high TYPE REF TO data, " Local Ref. Variable
         lrv_wa_l TYPE REF TO data, " Local Ref. Variable
         lrv_wa_h TYPE REF TO data, " Local Ref. Variable
         lrv_tmp  TYPE REF TO data. " Local Ref. Variable

  CONSTANTS : c_low(3) VALUE 'LOW'.

  FIELD-SYMBOLS : <fs_it>       TYPE STANDARD TABLE,

                  <fs>          TYPE any,
                  <fs_wa>       TYPE any,

                  <fs_sign>     TYPE any,
                  <fs_option>   TYPE any,
                  <fs_low>      TYPE any,
                  <fs_high>     TYPE any,
                  <fs_v_low>    TYPE any,
                  <fs_v_high>   TYPE any,
                  <fs_v_higher> TYPE any,
                  <fs_wa_low>   TYPE any,
                  <fs_wa_high>  TYPE any.


  CHECK t_itab[] IS NOT INITIAL.

  ASSIGN t_itab TO <fs>.
  CREATE DATA lrs_itab LIKE <fs>.
  CREATE DATA lrt_itab LIKE TABLE OF <fs>.

  ASSIGN lrs_itab->* TO <fs_wa>.
  ASSIGN lrt_itab->* TO <fs_it>.


**********************************************************************
  SORT t_itab BY (c_low).
  DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM t_itab COMPARING (c_low).
  READ TABLE t_itab ASSIGNING <fs_wa> INDEX 1.
  ASSIGN COMPONENT 'LOW' OF STRUCTURE <fs_wa> TO <fs_low>.
**********************************************************************


**********************************************************************
  CREATE DATA lrv_low  LIKE <fs_low>.
  CREATE DATA lrv_high LIKE <fs_low>.
  CREATE DATA lrv_tmp  LIKE <fs_low>.
  CREATE DATA lrv_wa_l  LIKE <fs_low>.
  CREATE DATA lrv_wa_h  LIKE <fs_low>.

  ASSIGN lrv_low->*  TO <fs_v_low>.
  ASSIGN lrv_high->* TO <fs_v_high>.
  ASSIGN lrv_tmp->*  TO <fs_v_higher>.
  ASSIGN lrv_wa_l->* TO <fs_wa_low>.
  ASSIGN lrv_wa_h->* TO <fs_wa_high>.
**********************************************************************

  <fs_v_low> = <fs_low> .

  PERFORM increase_1 USING <fs_low> CHANGING <fs_v_higher>.

  LOOP AT t_itab ASSIGNING <fs> FROM 2.
    ASSIGN COMPONENT 'LOW' OF STRUCTURE <fs> TO <fs_low>.
    ASSIGN COMPONENT 'SIGN'   OF STRUCTURE <fs> TO <fs_sign>.
    ASSIGN COMPONENT 'OPTION' OF STRUCTURE <fs> TO <fs_option>.

    IF <fs_sign>    IS NOT ASSIGNED OR
       <fs_option>  IS NOT ASSIGNED OR
       <fs_sign>    NE 'I' OR
       <fs_option>  NE 'EQ' .
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.


    IF <fs_low> EQ <fs_v_higher>.
      "Aralık değeri bulundu
      <fs_v_high> = <fs_low>.

      PERFORM increase_1 USING <fs_low> CHANGING <fs_v_higher>.

    ELSE.
      IF <fs_v_high> IS ASSIGNED AND
         <fs_v_high> IS NOT INITIAL.
        <fs_wa> = 'IBT'.
      ELSE .
        <fs_wa> = 'IEQ'.
      ENDIF.

      ASSIGN COMPONENT 'LOW'  OF STRUCTURE <fs_wa> TO <fs_wa_low>.
      ASSIGN COMPONENT 'HIGH' OF STRUCTURE <fs_wa> TO <fs_wa_high>.

      <fs_wa_low> = <fs_v_low>.
      <fs_wa_high> = <fs_v_high>.

      APPEND <fs_wa> TO <fs_it>.

      CLEAR: <fs_v_high>, <fs_wa>.

      <fs_v_low> = <fs_low>.
      PERFORM increase_1 USING <fs_low> CHANGING <fs_v_higher>.

    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.

  IF <fs_v_high> IS ASSIGNED AND
     <fs_v_high> IS NOT INITIAL.
    <fs_wa> = 'IBT'.
  ELSE .
    <fs_wa> = 'IEQ'.
  ENDIF.

  ASSIGN COMPONENT 'LOW'  OF STRUCTURE <fs_wa> TO <fs_wa_low>.
  ASSIGN COMPONENT 'HIGH' OF STRUCTURE <fs_wa> TO <fs_wa_high>.

  <fs_wa_low> = <fs_v_low>.
  <fs_wa_high> = <fs_v_high>.

  APPEND <fs_wa> TO <fs_it>.

  t_itab[] = <fs_it>.

ENDFUNCTION.


FORM increase_1 USING p_value CHANGING p_value_n.
  DATA: v_hx(1) TYPE x,
        v_len   TYPE i,
        v_off   TYPE i.

  p_value_n = p_value.

  FIELD-SYMBOLS <fs>.

*write:/ 'Initial:', p_value_n.
  v_len = strlen( p_value_n ).

  v_off = v_len - 1.

  DO v_len TIMES.
    IF p_value_n+v_off(1) BETWEEN 'A' AND 'Z'.
      IF p_value_n+v_off(1) LT 'Z'.
        SEARCH sy-abcde FOR p_value_n+v_off(1).
        DATA(lv_fdpos_next) = sy-fdpos + 1.
        p_value_n+v_off(1) = sy-abcde+lv_fdpos_next(1).
        EXIT.
      ELSE.
        p_value_n+v_off(1) = 'A'.
      ENDIF.
    ELSEIF p_value_n+v_off(1) BETWEEN '0' AND '9'.
      IF p_value_n+v_off(1) LT '9'.
        p_value_n+v_off(1) = p_value_n+v_off(1) + 1.
        EXIT.
      ELSE.
        p_value_n+v_off(1) = '0'.
      ENDIF.

    ELSE. "Will not work for special characters
      EXIT.
    ENDIF.
    v_off = v_off - 1.
  ENDDO.

*write:/ 'Final:', p_value_n.

ENDFORM.

 

Notes

The FM usage is pretty easy. Just call the FM in your development with the range table.

For standart programs, it has to be implemented with an enhancement or exit (if possible).

FM will run only for lines that has "I" as SIGN and "EQ" as OPTION.

Conclusion

This FM is a plug and play design. But If you have any question, please ask. I will be checking the blog for Q&A. I am looking forward to share and learn.

entry.title: Dynamically change the input type of a Parameters Variable.
entry.content:
Due to a particular requirement if a Basis layer decides to switch to a new table(Example: swu_wlscan2) instead of an old table(Example: swu_wlscan ) and thereby changing the value range of some of its Parameters(Example: Job Suffix), the application layer functionalities that use this table needs to adapt. The challenge here is that in application layer you are not sure that in a particular environment which basis release and support package is applicable and which of the 2 tables is active. In this case, if you have a program(Example/SAPSRM/OFFLINEAPPROVALSEND) in which there is Parameters declared for selection-screen and this Parameters type is that of the changed parameter in the Basis release, then there is problem statement of how to dynamically have this parameter type determined depending on the availability of the old or new table in Basis release. So that the application layer does not have to create a new program altogether to cater to the table changes.

You can solve the issue by

  1.     First replacing the static type declaration of Parameters to dynamic

Example:

     PARAMETERS: p_jobsuf LIKE SWU_WLSCAN-CURR_NUMB.

      Replace above with below statement

      PARAMETERS: p_jobsuf LIKE (lv_type). 

2.     Find the type of Dynamic type (Example:lv_type) in Initialization event block by checking the existence of the table.

INITIALIZATION.

CALL FUNCTION 'DD_EXIST_TABLE'
EXPORTING
  tabname      = 'SWU_WLSCAN2'
  status       = 'A'
IMPORTING
  subrc        = lv_subrc
EXCEPTIONS
  wrong_status = 1
  OTHERS       = 2.
IF lv_subrc <> 0.
  lv_type = 'SWU_WLSCAN-CURR_NUMB'.
ELSE.
  lv_type = 'SWU_WLSCAN2-CURR_NUMB'.
ENDIF.

entry.title: SAP ABAP Training und Zertifizierung: Transformation und Zukunftsausrichtung
entry.content:
SAP ABAP – was ist neu?

ABAP (Advanced Business Application Programming) ist eine proprietäre Programmier-sprache, die von SAP entwickelt wurde und seit den 1980er Jahren in der SAP-Umgebung verwendet wird. Sie ist vor allem für die Entwicklung von Anwendungen innerhalb der SAP ERP- und S/4HANA-Plattformen zuständig.

ABAP hat sich im Laufe der Jahre kontinuierlich weiterentwickelt, insbesondere mit der Einführung neuer Technologien und Anforderungen im Markt. Der Wechsel zu ABAP Cloud ist dabei mehr als nur ein technologisches Upgrade, denn dieser Schritt treibt Effizienz und Innovation voran und stellt sicher, dass Unternehmen wettbewerbsfähig und zukunftssicher bleiben.

Ab 2024 gibt es Neuerungen rund um generative KI wie den SAP Build und Extensibility Wizard, Integration in SAP S/4HANA, SDKs für Cloud-Integrationen und vieles mehr. Details und weitere Quellen gibt es dazu in der SAP ABAP Community.

SAP ABAP Schulungen – Updates 2024

Da sich so viel technologisch geändert hat, mussten auch die ABAP Schulungen transformiert werden. Dieser Blog beruht unter anderem auf einem Webinar dazu. Die Transformation des ABAP Trainings soll sicherstellen, dass die Entwickler, Berater, Admins und andere die mit ABAP arbeiten über aktuelle und relevante Kenntnisse verfügen.

Menschen Lernen ganz unterschiedlich – daher haben wir auch unterschiedliche Lernangebote. Von Selbstlerneinheiten, Live-Sessions bis hin zu Schulungen die von Trainern durchgeführt werden.

Kostenfreie Angebote zum selbstgesteuerten Lernen auf learning.sap.com:

  • Setting up an ABAP Environment on SAP BTP 
  • Learning the Basics of ABAP Programming on SAP BTP
  • Acquiring Core ABAP Skills
  • Practicing Clean Core Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Getting Started with SAP Forms services by Adobe

Neue Trainergeführte ABAP Schulungen – virtuell oder im Klassenzimmer

  • S4D400 - Basic ABAP Programming | SAP Training
  • S4D401 - Intermediate ABAP Programming | SAP Training
  • S4D430 - Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services | SAP Training
  • S4D425 - Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA | SAP Training
  • S4D437 - Transactional Apps with the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model | SAP Training
  • S4D440 - Custom Code Migration from SAP ERP to SAP S/4HANA | SAP Training

ABAP Zertifizierung

Lernende, die ihre ABAP-Expertise validieren möchten, können unsere neue rollenbasierte Zertifizierung absolvieren: SAP Certified Associate - Back-End Developer - ABAP Cloud

Wir schlagen dazu ein Abo des SAP Learning Hubs vor. Denn dort sind bereits 4 Zertifizierungsversuche inkludiert, sowie Assessments für die jährliche Rezertifizierung (Stay Certified), Live Sessions mit regelmässigen Experten-Updates auch zu Technologie-Themen wie ABAP, Schulungssysteme zum Üben in vorkonfiguerierten Systemen, Lerncommunities und auch deutschsprachige Lern & Info-Ressourcen.

Vergleich alter und neuer ABAP Kurse

Anbei eine andere Darstellung für alle, die noch die alten Kursnamen kennen. Ein Beispiel ist der BC400 - ein legendärer Kurs, den wohl die meisten SAP Experten einmal besucht haben.

  • BC100 Einführung in die Programmierung mit ABAP (Nachfolger: Learning the Basics of ABAP Programming on SAP BTP S4D100)
  • BC400 ABAP Workbench Grundlagen - SAP Business Suite (Nachfolger: S4D400 Basic ABAP Programming )
  • BC401 ABAP-Objekte (Nachfolger: S4D400 Basic ABAP Programming sowie S4D401 Intermediate ABAP Programming)
  • BC402 Advanced ABAP (Kein Nachfolger verfügbar)
  • BC405 Programmierung von ABAP-Reports (Nachfolger: S4D430 Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services)
  • BC410 Programmieren von Benutzerdialogen mit klassischen Bildern (Dynpros) mit den Elementen (Kein Nachfolger verfügbar)
  • NET310 Fundamentals of Web Dynpro for ABAP und NET313 Floorplan Manager for Web Dynpro ABAP (Kein Nachfolger verfügbar)
  • BC414 Programmieren von Datenbankaktualisierungen (Nachfolger: S4D437 Transactional Apps with the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model)
  • BC425 Erweiterungen und Modifikationen (Nachfolger: S4D425 Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA)
  • BC430 ABAP Dictionary (Nachfolger: S4D430 Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services)
  • BC481 Interaktive Formulare (Nachfolger: S4D480 PDF-Based Print Forms with SAP S/4HANA)
  • D75AW Delta ABAP Workbench SAP NetWeaver 7.0 bis SAP NetWeaver 7.51 (Nachfolger: S4D400 Basic ABAP Programming, S4D401 Intermediate ABAP Programming sowie S4D430 Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services)
  • S4C81 Erweiterungen mit der ABAP-Umgebung von SAP S/4HANA Cloud entwickeln (Nachfolger: S4D426)

Hinweis: Für einige Kurse (BC402, BC410, NET310, NET313) sind keine direkten Nachfolger verfügbar.

SAP ABAP Lernen - Praktische Tipps:

  • Kombinieren Sie selbstgesteuertes Lernen mit Trainer geführten Trainings, Live-Sessions und Schulungs-Systemen (Hands-on-Practice) je nach Lernpräferenzen
  • Halten Sie Ihre Zertifizierungen durch jährliche Delta-Assessments aktuell
  • Nutzen Sie die Vorteilsangebote rund um SAP Training: ob kundenindividuelle Trainings, Volumenrabatte wie SAP Preferred Card

Zusammenfassung

SAP transformiert das ABAP-Training und die Zertifizierungen, um den Übergang zu ABAP in der Cloud zu unterstützen und auf die wachsende Nachfrage nach Cloud-Kompetenzen zu reagieren. Ab dem Juli 2024 wurden die 12 klassische Präsenzschulungen durch neue Kurse ersetzt, die sich auf ABAP in der Cloud, Clean Core und S/4HANA Extensibility konzentrieren.

Entwickler und technische SAP Experten können ihre Fähigkeiten durch verschiedene Lernpfade erweitern, einschließlich kostenloser Learning Journeys auf learning.sap.com und Instructor-Led Trainings.

Die neue ABAP Cloud-Zertifizierung und regelmäßige Updates durch Delta-Assessments stellen sicher, dass Entwickler mit den neuesten Technologien Schritt halten.

Diese Transformation zielt darauf ab, Partnern und Entwicklern zukunftssichere Fähigkeiten zu vermitteln und sie für die sich wandelnde IT-Landschaft zu rüsten. 

Fragen und Antworten aud dem Webinar rund um ABAP

 

Frage

Antwort

Kann man in SAP Learning Hub mit Eclipse üben? wenn ja, welchen Kurs?

Eclipse ist spätestens mit S/4 die präferierte Entwicklungsumgebung für ABAP. Bestimmte Entwicklungobjekte lassen sich nicht mehr im GUI (se80) pflegen. Um ABAP in Eclipse entwickeln zu können, muss man dort ein Plug-In der SAP installieren, die ABAP Development Tools (ADT). Früher hatten wir eine eigene BC*-Schulung im Angebot, die explizit das Arbeiten mit ADT behandelte. Im Bereich der S4D*-Kurse bieten wir so etwas nicht mehr an. Stattdessen schulen wir ADT “by doing”. D.h. in so gut wie allen S4D*-Schulungen wird ADT als Entwicklungsumgebung verwendet. Die Teilnehmer lernen ADT damit im Zusammenhang mit bestimmten ABAP Themen kennen, wie etwa ABAP Core Data Services (CDS) oder ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP).

Kann ich denn auf der learning.sap.com coden?

Auf learning.sap.com kann man über Hands-On Practice Learning Systeme finden, diese sind dann im Learning Hub Subscription enthalten.

Was ist denn die Zertifizierung zu den ILT-Angeboten?

Egal ob ILT oder Self-Paced eLearning, die Zerti für ABAP bleibt gleich: C_ABAPD. https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-back-end-developer-abap-cloud

Hi Caroline, ich wünsche mir als Unternehmensberater einen BTP Lernpfad 🙂 Ist sowas angedacht? Lieben Gruß

Wir bieten für SAP BTP viele Schulungen an - Self-Paced und als ILT. Startpunkt wäre: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/discover-sap-business-technology-platform Weitere Learning Journeys als inspiration: https://learning.sap.com/products/business-technology-platform.

50% der Kunden wollen wohl nicht in die Cloud, aber ABAP wird Cloud only? Bzw. Innovation nur noch in Cloud? Was ist, wenn wer nicht mitgeht, gilt dann private Cloud? https://www.heise.de/news/Anwender-fremdeln-weiterhin-mit-SAPs-Cloud-Strategie-9663103.html

Die Frage ist nicht specific zu Learning sondern die SAP Strategie. Wichtig aus ABAP sicht - On Prem oder Cloud, der Code bleibt gleich. ABAP Cloud ist ein Entwicklungsmodell was auch On-Premise benutzt werden kann.

Meine Zertifizierung ist schon 8 Jahre her, Kann ich diese ebenfalls mit der Delta Variante updaten?

Ja, das ist möglich.

Kann man SAP Zertifizierungen von einem Business SAP Account zu einem privaten Account umziehen, für den Fall, dass man die Firma wechselt?

Dazu bitte das SAP Learning Support Center besuchen: https://support.learning.sap.com

 

Weitere Informationen rund um ABAP Training

  • Mehr Einzelheiten zur Transformation des ABAP Curriculum finden Sie in den FAQ.
  • Die Aufnahme dieser und anderer Webinare rund um SAP Training und Zertifizierung im Herbst 2024 finden Sie hier.
  • Abonnieren Sie den SAP Training Newsletter um monatlich up-to-date zu bleiben.

entry.title: Driving Innovation with SAP Build at SAP TechEd 2024
entry.content:
SAP TechEd 2024 delivered a series of transformative announcements, setting the stage for how developers and businesses will innovate using SAP technology. With a strong focus on generative AI and advancements in SAP Build, this year's event underscored SAP's commitment to driving next-generation business transformation.

Here is a comprehensive look at the major highlights for SAP Build from this year's SAP TechEd.

 

SAP Build: Streamlining Development Across the Board

One of the most significant advancements from SAP TechEd 2024 is the expansion of SAP Build. SAP Build enables developers to extend and create applications on SAP S/4HANA Cloud and other line-of-business applications seamlessly. SAP's new extensibility wizard allows developers to create extensions from S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with SAP Build, while maintaining critical business context like objects, events, and data. This feature ensures that extensions, whether developed side-by-side or on-stack, preserve essential business logic without data loss.

Developers will also be able to leverage the extensibility wizard to create SAP Fiori and SAPUI5 applications directly within SAP Build, reducing time-to-market for new apps and simplifying workflows.

Last but certainly not least, SAP announced the integration of ABAP development environments within SAP Build. By the end of 2024, developers will be able to access ABAP tools from the SAP Build Lobby, the platform's central hub for all development projects. This update makes SAP Build a unified development environment, allowing fusion teams to collaborate seamlessly across multiple technology stacks. Teams can now move effortlessly between SAP Build Apps, SAP Build Code, and ABAP environments, driving faster innovation.

For a more comprehensive view of what this looks like for developers, click here.

 

Generative AI: Joule and Beyond

Generative AI was a central theme through SAP TechEd 2024 with new capabilities designed to make developers' lives easier and applications smarter. Joule, SAP's generative AI assistant, is set to expand its capabilities across the SAP Build portfolio, providing AI-powered task automation, intelligent support within development environments, and more.

In 2025, ABAP developers will gain the ability to use Joule to generate high-quality code and unit tests that adhere to ABAP Cloud development standards. Joule will also assist with legacy code explanations, helping developers modernize outdated systems and accelerate the transition to clean core.

Developers can also look forward to Joule Studio, a new platform for building custom AI skills for Joule. These skills can be integrated into S/4HANA Cloud, allowing businesses to create bespoke AI-powered workflows tailored to their specific needs. Joule's generative AI capabilities will soon extend beyond SAP environments, allowing developers to integrate non-SAP applications with ease.

SAP also introduced new features in the Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core, with models like IBM Granite, DALL·E 3, and Amazon Titan, coming to the platform by the end of 2024. These tools will empower developers to create AI-driven applications, including image generation and sophisticated AI orchestration workflows. Generative AI Hub will also offer prompt templates, data masking, and orchestration capabilities to streamline AI implementation across various use cases. 

 

What SAP TechEd 2024 Means for Developers

SAP TechEd 2024 showcased a clear vision for the future, with innovations in SAP Build and generative AI that will reshape how developers and businesses approach enterprise applications. With the continuous expansion of AI-driven features like Joule and the unified development environments, SAP is positioning itself at the forefront of digital transformation.

As we move into 2025, developers can expect even more opportunities to leverage coding capabilities of all skillsets and AI to create smarter, more efficient business solutions. Whether you're a seasoned developer or just beginning your journey with SAP, the new tools and enhancements announced at TechEd will empower you to build the future of enterprise technology.

Start exploring these powerful new capabilities today by signing up for an SAP BTP Trial account and take your first step toward building the future of enterprise applications.

entry.title: Avoid Overengineering in Software Development: Make it simple with KISS,DRY,YAGNI and MVP
entry.content:
Overengineering in software development is like building a Swiss Army knife when all you need is a simple screwdriver. It happens when developers create overly complex solutions for problems that could have been solved more simply. While the intentions behind overengineering might be good, such as aiming for flexibility, scalability, or robustness, it often leads to unnecessary complexity, increased costs, and slower development cycles.

I’m obviously a fan of clean code principles 🙈😅. In this blog, i will highlight the overengineering practices in SAP development and introduce you to practical strategies like DRY,KISS,YANGI, and maybe MVP that will help you avoid falling into the trap of over-engineering.

 

What Is Overengineering?

Overengineering occurs when developers build solutions that are far more complicated than required for the current problem. This could mean adding extra features, implementing complex architecture, or overgeneralizing code to cater to future needs that may never arise.

some examples from our SAP life :

  • Building overly complex custom ABAP modules when SAP S/4HANA already provides standard functionality to meet the requirement.
  • Implementing complex logic within SAP Fiori/freestyle UI5 apps when a simple Fiori Elements standard list report/object page App could deliver the required business value.

While it may seem prudent to "future-proof" the solution, this can backfire, leading to inflated development and maintenance efforts 😅.

The Hidden Cost of Overengineering

SAP implementations are often complex due to the nature of the enterprise environments they serve. SAP landscapes typically include a mix of complex integrated platforms, cloud services, and more. In such environments, developers are often tempted to create highly flexible and future-proof systems to meet all possible requirements. While this approach may seem like a safeguard against future changes, it can quickly lead to various tangible and intangible costs:

  • Increased Complexity: Complex solutions require more time to develop, test, and maintain, which can lead to budget overruns and timeline delays.
  • Reduced Agility: When a system is overly complex, it becomes harder to make changes quickly in response to new business requirements.
  • Higher Maintenance Costs: Overengineered solutions often require more effort to maintain, especially when custom code replaces standard SAP functionality.
  • Frustrated Business Stakeholders: Users may become frustrated when they see delays and costs rising while key functionalities are being deprioritized in favor of future-proofing that may never be needed.

YAGNI: You Aren't Gonna Need It

The YAGNI principle, which originated from the Extreme Programming (XP) methodology, advises developers not to implement functionality until it is absolutely necessary. It encourages a lean and focused approach, where only the most immediate and critical features are built.

In SAP projects, YAGNI can help avoid the tendency to add unnecessary complexity:

  • Avoid speculative features: SAP developers, especially when working with custom ABAP or UI5 applications, often anticipate potential future requirements. However, implementing such features too early can lead to bloated codebases. For example, designing overly generic data models or complex business logic for hypothetical use cases can complicate future enhancements.
  • Simplify development: By focusing on what is needed now, SAP teams can deliver faster and with more agility. This is particularly important in the rapidly changing enterprise environment, where business needs are often volatile. Whether it's developing custom apps using SAP Fiori or creating custom integrations in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), YAGNI encourages the team to focus on real requirements, not assumptions.
  • Maintainability: With fewer unnecessary features, the SAP landscape remains easier to maintain and upgrade over time, reducing technical debt and improving the long-term ROI of the project.

MVP: Minimum Viable Product

MVP focuses on delivering the simplest version of a product that meets the core needs of users, allowing teams to validate their ideas quickly.

In my opinion, MVP approach is ideal for testing new ideas, especially when you have a concept you want to bring to market or when you’re operating within a budget and need a quick, straightforward solution

Example: Developing a  SAP Fiori application that is intended to manage employee requests for leave. Instead of building an extensive system with complex reporting, notifications, and user roles, the MVP could focus solely on the essential features: a simple user interface for submitting leave requests and a basic admin interface for approving them. This allows for quick feedback from users, enabling the team to prioritize additional features based on real needs rather than assumptions.

DRY: Don’t Repeat Yourself

The DRY principle in my opinion is the main approach to better code refactoring since it aligns with the clean code principles that encourage avoiding code duplication by abstracting and reusing code wherever possible. Popularized by the book, The Pragmatic Programmer, the DRY principle states that, “every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous, authoritative representation within a system.” Using the principle, logic, or algorithms that have certain functionality should only appear once in an application. This also includes refraining from using the same solution or approach multiple times to solve the same problem. If there are multiple solutions to a problem, try to consolidate them into one. This leads to a cleaner and more maintainable codebase.

Example: In an SAP ABAP program, if multiple reports are generating similar output formats (e.g., customer invoices), rather than duplicating the code for formatting in each report, developers can create a single reusable function module. This modular approach reduces redundancy, simplifies future updates (since changes need to be made in only one place), and helps maintain consistency across the application.

KISS: Keep It Simple, Stupid

In software development, code refactoring is an ongoing journey! In my opinion, the ideal code is the kind that’s self-explanatory and easy to understand even for a junior developer. The KISS principle advocates for simplicity in design and implementation. Solutions should be as simple as possible without unnecessary complexity.

Example: Suppose you are developing a custom SAP UI5 application for tracking sales orders. Instead of implementing multiple complicated user interfaces with various navigation paths, a KISS approach would mean creating a straightforward layout with a single page for viewing and updating orders. This not only makes it easier for users to understand but also simplifies the development and maintenance processes.

 

Practical Tips for Avoiding Overengineering :

  • Conduct Regular Requirement Reviews: Maintain open lines of communication with stakeholders to ensure that requirements are clear and current. Regularly revisit these requirements to adapt to changes in the business landscape.
  • Prioritize Core Functionality: When designing solutions, focus on the features that provide the most value to users. Defer any non-essential features to future iterations.
  • Use Prototyping: Develop prototypes to test ideas before committing to full-scale implementation. This can help validate assumptions and reduce the risk of building unnecessary complexity.
  • Emphasize Documentation: Maintain clear and concise documentation of requirements, design decisions, and code. This can help prevent miscommunication and ensure that all team members are aligned on project goals.
  • Encourage Team Collaboration: Foster a culture of collaboration within your development team, encouraging open discussions about design choices and potential complexities. This collective wisdom can help identify and eliminate over-engineering tendencies.

Conclusion

Over-engineering is a common challenge in software development, but it can be avoided by adopting principles like DRY,KISS,YANGI and MVP , my Final word is here that, continued code refactoring is part of the game but always try to keep it as simple as possible.

“So if you want to go fast, if you want to get done quickly, if you want your code to be easy to write, make it easy to read.” ― Robert C. Martin, Clean Code: A Handbook of Agile Software Craftsmanship. The Clean Code Lesson 1 by Uncle Bob 😉   

 

Happy coding 🤗 😉!!

entry.title: The next step of ABAPs evolution: ABAP Cloud now part of SAP Build
entry.content:
"We’ve always defined ourselves by the ability to overcome the impossible." (Movie: Interstellar, 2014)

Here we are again, with the next step in the evolution of ABAP.

ABAP Cloud

It’s been six years since SAP launched the so-called SAP Cloud Platform ABAP Environment, which allowed developers to build and run ABAP code in the cloud. Now called SAP BTP ABAP Environment, it is the SAP Platform-as-a-Service offering that enables developers to leverage their traditional ABAP skills to build and run ABAP applications in the cloud on SAP BTP, either as an extension to SAP S/4HANA (any edition) or as standalone applications. With the SAP BTP ABAP environment, we are targeting business application developers who have extensive SAP experience and domain knowledge, particularly around SAP ERP solutions. See Steampunk is going all-in and and It's Steampunk now, to read about it’s history.

Derived from this, something ingenious was introduced: The ABAP Cloud development model. ABAP Cloud is a perfect example of how innovation works. The story of ABAP Cloud began quite silent. In fact, ABAP Cloud was born in the SAP BTP ABAP environment, where  it’s the leading and only development model available for ABAP development. It’s the amazing result of the ABAP engineering team, which has evolved ABAP into ABAP Cloud, making it future-proof, upgrade-stable and, you could say, cloud native. 

With the ABAP Cloud development model, you can build cloud-ready and upgrade-stable business applications, services, and extensions. ABAP Cloud was introduced in the BTP ABAP environment, but like any other innovation success story, ABAP Cloud has expanded its home turf, it has grown in both scope and availability.

That’s how the idea was born to make ABAP Cloud available everywhere.

The result is that ABAP Cloud is already the underlying ABAP development model in SAP BTP ABAP environment and in S/4HANA Cloud, public edition as of 2208 (3 system landscape required). Additionally, it can be used in S/4HANA Cloud, private edition as well as in S/4HANA any premise from >2022. We highly recommend using ABAP Cloud already for your S/4HANA private cloud as well as S/4HANA on premise projects to set the guardrails to reach a clean core and preparing your cloud journey.

 

Picture 1: ABAP Cloud Availability

SAP Build 

Back to the opening statement: the next step in ABAP’s evolution. ABAP Cloud is now part of SAP Build! This is the amazing success story of how true integration works. How technology can overcome the borders of more than 40 years of evolutionary history. For more details on this great enhancement, read this technical blog post by Michael Ameling.

But what happens when the paths of SAP Build and ABAP Cloud cross to form the world’s strongest alliance for customer and partner development in the SAP ecosystem? What does this mean for you, our developer community?

With SAP Build, you can accelerate your ERP modernization, foster innovation and automate processes, all within a single comprehensive suite of extension development tools that includes low-code, pro-code and generative AI capabilities.

SAP Build provides you the flexibility to create and extend applications, automate processes and design digital workspaces with low-code, pro-code and generative AI tools. Its collaborative design allows diverse teams to build solutions faster using pre-built solutions, AI assistance, and seamless integration with SAP applications.​

It has never been easier from a developer’s perspective, since SAP Build provides you all the tools you need in one place,  so you can focus on the real development to be done. The SAP Build Lobby is the starting point for your Java/JavaScript projects and your ABAP Cloud projects.

Picture 2: SAP Build Lobby

Our goal is to provide you with a seamless development flow, regardless of the environment you’re working in most of the time. Examples of this integration include

  • The tool bridge between the ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse (ADT) and SAP Business Application Studio: The tool bridge connects ADT and BAS, which connects the front-end with the back-end development. It keeps the context of the services and entities as input for the Fiori generator.
  • The SAP Build Lobby as a single-entry point : You can start your ABAP development project from the lobby next to other build extension development options. You can access a list of ABAP systems directly from the lobby and keep a project reference to the ABAP system and development package.
  • The Graphical Modeler for ABAP: You can reuse CAP project models to generate ABAP Core Data Services (ABAP CDS) from it.   

In addition to all the benefits mentioned, it doesn’t matter if you’re building on-stack extensions in S/4HANA Cloud or side-by-side extensions on SAP BTP: SAP Build is the umbrella.

Picture 3: Extensibility Portfolio for S/4HANA public cloud edition

And this is only the beginning: We aim to integrate developer tools and capabilities into a seamless user centered offering and provide a clear developer tools strategy, so you can start building your SAP projects right away. Including ABAP Cloud in SAP Build is a testament to you as an ABAP Cloud developer. Your skills are more than important and strategic in the SAP application development and automation portfolio.

That is already exciting. And we haven’t even covered Joule’s capabilities in detail so far in this blog post.

We aim to increase the developer efficiency across the entire development lifecycle through generative AI and Joule embedded in all our IDEs and tools. Stay tuned for more news to come, especially on GenAI.

Resources

learn more about the topic:

  • Public Roadmap for SAP BTP, ABAP Environment
  • ABAP Cloud - Technical use cases and recommended technologies
  • Practicing Clean Core Extensibility For SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • SAP Certified Associate - Back-End Developer - ABAP Cloud
  • Extend SAP S/4HANA in the cloud and on premise with ABAP based extensions
  • ABAP Cloud FAQ
  • Building Low Code Extensions with Key User Extensibility in SAP S/4HANA and SAP Build
  • Extend SAP S/4HANA with SAP Build
  • Clean core extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud
